coredata.dc:description: Antibiotics are emerging hazardous small molecules, requiring urgent need for fast signal-on analytical methods to control antibiotic abuse. Signal-on fluorescence sensing strategies utilizing nanoquenchers and aptamers are fascinating but rarely accomplished on paper-based platforms. Here, a novel multilayer MXene sensing platform was established by leveraging Nb2C-MXene as a multilayer nanoquencher and zero-dimensional carbon dots-labeled aptamer (B-CDs@Apt) as a stable and bright recognition probe. The Nb2C-MXene has a multilayer nanosheet stack-like structure and efficient mass transfer channels. It can efficiently adsorb abundant B-CDs@Apt probes and quench their fluorescence. Importantly, the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system can release the B-CDs@Apt in response to the analyte with high sensitivity, thereby restoring the fluorescent signal. The developed aptasensor achieved sensitive and selective detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) and showed satisfactory anti-interference ability, stability, and practicability. Notably, the multilayer Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system was successfully transferred to a paper-based sensing platform, with a low-density distribution of multilayer nanoquenchers carrying sufficient aptamer probes for analyte access. In comparison, the monolayer Nb2C nanosheets were unable to adsorb enough probes to output analyte-induced signals. The established paper-based analytical device (PAD) showed a LOD of 0.360 ng mL−1 for CAP, which is the first paper-based MXene aptasensor reported for fluorescence detection. By replacing the aptamer and carbon dot, the strategy was further extended to detect another analyte oxytetracycline (OTC), with LODs of 0.399 in tube and 0.867 ng mL−1 on PAD, respectively. Furthermore, concurrent detection of CAP and OTC was achieved using a dual-color PAD, demonstrating the potential to meet multi-target analytical requirements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram. (a) Illustration of the preparation of Nb2C-MXene and B-CDs@Apt. (b) Schematic drawing of the working principle of the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt aptasensor. (c) Microscopic schematic of the paper-based analytical device constructed using multilayer MXene and carbon dots-functionalized aptamer. (d) Illustration of the advantages of the multilayer MXene system on paper due to enhanced probe loading efficiency compared to traditional nanoquenchers such as monolayer nanosheets.

Characterization of the synthesis of Nb2C-MXene. (a) SEM image of Nb2C-MXene. (b–e) EDS mapping images of Nb2C-MXene [Nb (yellow), C (red), O (green), and Al (brown)]. (f) SEM image of Nb2AlC precursor. (g) EDS mapping images of Nb2AlC precursor [Nb (yellow), C (red), O (green), and Al (brown)]. (h) PXRD patterns of Nb2C-MXene and Nb2AlC precursor. (i) UV–vis spectrum of Nb2C-MXene.

Characterization of the synthesized B-CDs. (a) HRTEM analysis of B-CDs. Inset: HRTEM image showing the interplanar lattice spacing. (b) Particle size distribution diagram of B-CDs. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra (black line), excitation spectra (red line) and emission spectra (blue line) of B-CDs. Inset: Photographs of B-CDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1) under daylight and UV light (365 nm). (d–f) FT-IR spectra of (d) B-CDs, (e) L-Glutamic acid, and (f) o-phenylenediamine. (g) XPS spectrum of B-CDs. (h–j) XPS spectra of (h) C1s, (i) N1s, and (j) O1s for B-CDs.

Feasibility (a–d), optimization (e–h), and performance (i–l) of the aptasensor. (a) Zeta potentials of B-CDs, B-CDs@Apt, Nb2C-MXene and Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt mixture. (b) Emission spectra of B-CDs@Apt, Nb2C-MXene, Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system, and the system after incubation with CAP (150 ng mL−1). (c) Emission spectra of B-CDs and Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs mixture. (d) Images under UV light (365) nm of a filter paper fully covered with Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt material (22.5 μg/300 pmol) before and after addition of CAP (150 ng mL−1). (e) Effect of Nb2C-MXene concentration on the fluorescence intensity of the B-CDs@Apt. (f) Effects of pH of the buffer on the fluorescence intensities of the B-CDs, B-CDs@Apt, and Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system. Effects of (g) incubation time and (h) incubation temperature after adding the CAP (300 ng mL−1) on the fluorescence intensity of the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system. (Excitation/emission wavelength: 350/420 nm). (i) Emission spectra of Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system after adding different concentrations of CAP. (j) A linear calibration curve depicting the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the CAP concentration. (k) Fluorescence responses of Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt aptasensor for CAP and other antibiotics (the concentration of CAP was 150 ng mL−1, while the concentrations of other antibiotics were 1.5 μg mL−1). (l) Evaluation of stability of the signal-on aptasensor everyday in a week.

Detection and analysis of CAP in spiked aquatic samples. (a) Heat-map visualization of CAP detected via the proposed Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt aptasensor and LC–MS/MS in different aquatic products. (b–d) Consistency test between the aptasensor and LC–MS/MS in quantifying CAP in (b) shrimp, (c) mackerel, and (d) codfish samples. (Pearson's r: Pearson correlation coefficient; CCC: concordance correlation coefficient). (e–g) Bland–Altman analysis between aptasensor and LC–MS/MS in quantifying CAP in (e) shrimp, (f) mackerel, and (g) codfish samples.

Solid state detection using the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt paper-based sensor. (a) Schematic diagram of the fabrication protocol and detection process of the paper-based analysis device. (b) Fluorescent image of the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt-loaded MCE films with sample added and the corresponding plot of B/R value versus the concentration of CAP. (c) Linear calibration curve between B/R value and logarithmic CAP concentration corresponds to the (b) plot. Reaction time: 25 min (b–c). (d–e) SEM images of the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt-loaded MCE films at (d) 10 and (e) 1 μm scales. (f) Illustration of multilayer Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt loaded on round MCE paper (12.5 mm2). (g–h) Illustration of monolayer Nb2C-MXene nanosheets/B-CDs@Apt loaded on round MCE paper (12.5 mm2) and fluorescent image of the papers with sample added. Mass concentration of the monolayer Nb2C-MXene nanosheets: 750 ng (g) and 75 ng (h).

Method extension and duplex detection study. (a) Schematic diagram of the method extension. (b) Emission spectra of G-CDs@Apt, Nb2C-MXene, Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt system, and the system after incubation with OTC (200 ng mL−1). (c) Emission spectra of Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt system after adding different concentrations of OTC. (d) A linear calibration curve depicting the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the OTC concentration. (e) Fluorescent image of the Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt-loaded MCE films with OTC added and the corresponding plot of G/R value versus the concentration of OTC. (f) Linear calibration curve between G/R value and logarithmic OTC concentration corresponds to the (e) plot. (g–h) Anti-interference test of MXene/CDs@Apt paper-based system to detect (g) CAP (100 ng/mL) and (h) OTC (50 ng/mL) against other antibiotics (10 ×). (i) Comparison of the emission spectra of B-CDs@Apt and G-CDs@Apt under 365 nm excitation. (j) Schematic diagram of the duplex detection of CAP and OTC. (k) Fluorescence image of duplex detection on a PAD under UV light (365 nm). (l) Normalized analysis of B/R value and G/R value in the results of (k) plot. (k–l) The dosage ratio of B-CDs@Apt and G-CDs@Apt was 1:3.

head.ce:title: Signal-on aptasensors on paper-based platform: Application of multilayer MXene nanoquencher and stabilized luminescent carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibiotics are emerging hazardous small molecules, requiring urgent need for fast signal-on analytical methods to control antibiotic abuse. Signal-on fluorescence sensing strategies utilizing nanoquenchers and aptamers are fascinating but rarely accomplished on paper-based platforms. Here, a novel multilayer MXene sensing platform was established by leveraging Nb2C-MXene as a multilayer nanoquencher and zero-dimensional carbon dots-labeled aptamer (B-CDs@Apt) as a stable and bright recognition probe. The Nb2C-MXene has a multilayer nanosheet stack-like structure and efficient mass transfer channels. It can efficiently adsorb abundant B-CDs@Apt probes and quench their fluorescence. Importantly, the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system can release the B-CDs@Apt in response to the analyte with high sensitivity, thereby restoring the fluorescent signal. The developed aptasensor achieved sensitive and selective detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) and showed satisfactory anti-interference ability, stability, and practicability. Notably, the multilayer Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system was successfully transferred to a paper-based sensing platform, with a low-density distribution of multilayer nanoquenchers carrying sufficient aptamer probes for analyte access. In comparison, the monolayer Nb2C nanosheets were unable to adsorb enough probes to output analyte-induced signals. The established paper-based analytical device (PAD) showed a LOD of 0.360 ng mL−1 for CAP, which is the first paper-based MXene aptasensor reported for fluorescence detection. By replacing the aptamer and carbon dot, the strategy was further extended to detect another analyte oxytetracycline (OTC), with LODs of 0.399 in tube and 0.867 ng mL−1 on PAD, respectively. Furthermore, concurrent detection of CAP and OTC was achieved using a dual-color PAD, demonstrating the potential to meet multi-target analytical requirements.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Broad-spectrum antibiotics had been widely used to treat animal infections owing to their cost-effective inhibition of bacterial protein biosynthesis [1–3]. However, excessive exposure to antibiotics can cause some serious effects on the environment and human health. For instance, chloramphenicol has a high ecotoxicity for aquatic organisms through specific protein control [4,5]. Chloramphenicol may have adverse effects to animals, the most severe of which is aplastic anemia associated with defects in red blood cell and leukocyte production, leading to blood disorders and high mortality [6]. In addition, some studies have shown that chloramphenicol is potentially genotoxic and carcinogenic [7]. To avoid the threat of antibiotic abuse to human health, many countries have restricted the use of hazardous antibiotics in animal feed products, e.g., the European Union has set the maximum residue limit of CAP at 0.3 μg kg−1 [8–10]. However, some hazardous antibiotics are still used illegally in aquaculture because of their low cost and high efficiency [11]. Antibiotic analysis is typically performed via instrumental analysis methods such as chromatography-mass spectrometry [12]. While highly accurate and reproducible, these methods have their drawbacks, including costly instruments and time-consuming separation steps required. Other standard methods for detecting antibiotics, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and conventional lateral flow immunoassay, typically use a signal-off mode of competitive immunoassay and have limitations in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and stability [13]. Signal-off readout mode (bright to dark) does not easily distinguish visually the change in fluorescence signal (bright slightly darker) caused by a low concentration of the target, thus limiting detection sensitivity. Therefore, it is still urgent to develop an efficient signal-on method for antibiotic detection.

Fluorescent paper-based analytical device (PAD) has drawn wide attention as a powerful tool for on-site detention because of its portability, high sensitivity, and cost effectiveness [14]. In recent years, nanoquencher-based signal-on fluorescent aptasensors offer a great opportunity to achieve efficient, portable and on-site detection [15]. These nanoquenchers are mainly nanosheets such as metal-organic framework [16], graphene oxide [17], cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) [18], etc., which adsorb fluorogen-modified aptamer probes and quench the fluorescence, whereas the presence of the analyte releases the probes, thus recovering the fluorescence. This signal-on sensing mode does not require other signal-enhancing steps such as nucleic acid amplification, but instead relies primarily on the interaction between the nanoquencher and the aptamer component to work. The monolayer nanosheets show good detection ability in liquid assay, but in solid-phase assay mode, these nanosheets can be unfavorable for immobilization due to easy bending. Especially when the monolayer nanoquencher is immobilized on a PAD, where the area available for immobilization is limited, the fluorescent probe carried by the monolayer nanosheets for release is easily insufficient to be read out by a conventional detector [19]. Therefore, the choice of nanoquenchers is crucial.

MXenes are emerging transition-metal carbides, nitrides or carbonitrides with two-dimensional (2D) layered structures [20]. They have good electrical conductivity in favor of photo-induced electron transfer, as well as the advantages of large surface area and numerous channels for mass transfer, which make them good candidates for the fabrication of fluorescent biosensors [21–25]. Recently, Luo et al. constructed a fluorosensor using MXene-nanosheets and FAM-labeled aptamer probes, achieving a detection limit of 38.9 fg mL−1 for SARS-CoV-2 spike protein and 7.2 copies for SARS-CoV-2 pseudovirus [26]. Nevertheless, research on such MXene-based fluorescence aptasensors is still in its infancy, with rare solid-phase detection systems available and only conventional organic dyes-labeled aptamer probes being used.

Given the tantalizing idea of loading multilayer 2D MXene onto a PAD, we deliberately introduced nanomodification engineering to obtain stabilized luminescent aptamer probes. Conventional organic dyes are often limited in biosensing and paper-based applications owing to their small Stokes shifts and susceptibility to photobleaching [27]. As a novel class of zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention because of their distinct characteristics such as outstanding photoluminescent properties, excellent photostability, simplicity in synthesis, good water solubility, and high biocompatibility, which make them promising candidates for fluorescence labeling and biosensing [28]. Especially for the nanomodification engineering on aptamer, their size of less than 10 nm and 0D characteristics allow carbon dot-labeled aptamer probes to better maintain their original recognition function and free dispersion state compared to other larger-sized fluorescent nanoparticles [29]. Compared with traditional quantum dots, which contain heavy metal elements such as lead and cadmium, carbon dots are mainly composed of carbon, making them safer and more environmentally friendly [30]. Thereby, it is a tempting idea to combine MXene materials with carbon dot-labeled recognition probes to build robust biosensors. To our knowledge, fluorescence detection approaches based on MXene/carbon dot-integrated aptamer sensing platforms have seldom been explored.

Herein, we propose the use of multilayer MXene nanoquencher in combination with carbon dots-functionalized aptamer to create a novel signal-on detection platform. We report on the design and validation of the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt biosensor for CAP quantification. The increase in the fluorescence intensity signal of the biosensor was directly proportional to the concentration of the analyte CAP. The linear range was 1–160 ng mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.285 ng mL−1. Its application for shrimp and fish samples analysis exhibits satisfactory results. Importantly, the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt biosensor can be easily converted to a paper-based platform for on-site detection. Our ready-to-use test paper achieved a rapid and low-cost detection of CAP with a detection limit of 0.360 ng mL−1. To our knowledge, this is the first paper-based MXene aptasensor reported for fluorescence detection. Moreover, we extended the strategy to build another biosensor for oxytetracycline (OTC) by replacing the aptamer and carbon dot. The success of the duplex detection assay suggests that this scalable approach has the potential to be further developed into a multiplex detection platform.

L-Glutamic acid, o-phenylenediamine, formamide, acetonitrile, methanol, ethyl acetate were purchased from Sinopharm Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloramphenicol, oxytetracycline, ampicillin (AMP), norfloxacin (NOR), roxithromycin (ROX), streptomycin (STR), vancomycin (VAN) and erythromycin (ERY) were provided by Solarbio Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Niobium carbide aluminum (Nb2AlC) powder, nitrofurazone (NFZ), nitrofurantoin (NFT), furazolidone (FZD) were purchased from McLean Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), PBS buffer, Tris-HCl, magnesium chloride, sodium chloride, potassium chloride, and hydrofluoric acid (HF) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The 5′-amino-modified aptamers used in this study were synthesized and purified with HPLC by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The sequences are as follows: 5′-NH2-C6-CGT ACG GTC GAC GCT AGC TTA GCT TAT GCG TTG GCC GGG ATA AGG ATC CAG CCG TTG TAG ATT TGC GTT CTA ACT CTC CAC GTG GAG CTC GGA TCC-3′ (anti-CAP aptamer); 5′-NH2-C6-CGT ACG GAA TTC GCT AGC CGA GTT GAG CCG GGC GCG GTA CGG GTA CTG GTA TGT GTG GGG ATC CGA GCT CCA CGT G −3′ (anti-OTC aptamer). The dialysis bag (MW = 1000 Da) was purchased from Beijing Boaotoda Technology Co., Ltd., and the ultrafiltration tube (30 kDa) was purchased from Merck Millipore Co., Ltd. (Billerica, MA, USA). Mixed cellulose ester (MCE) films and cellulose acetate (CA) films were obtained from Jinteng Experiment Equipment Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Whatman Grade 1 filter papers were purchased from Whatman Co., Ltd. (Maidstone, UK). Green fluorescence carbon dots were purchased from Mesolight Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China). Monolayer Nb2C-MXene nanosheets were purchased from Beike 2D materials Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water (≥18 MΩ cm) purified by a Milli-Q water purification system was used throughout the experiment.

The morphologies and structures of Nb2C-MXene were examined by using a Regulus 8100 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan). Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data were recorded by an Ultima IV (Rigaku Corporation, Japan) equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15405 nm, 40KV, 40 mA). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of B-CDs were obtained by a JEM-F200 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were collected on a Tu-1901 spectrophotometer (BEIJING PUXI GENERAL INSTRUMENT, China). The fluorescence spectra were measured on an F97Pro spectrofluorometer (Lengguang Technology, China). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a PerkinElmer Spectrum 3 Infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded using an ESCALAB 250XI Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) analysis was performed using an ACQUITY UPLC H-CLASS_XEVO TQ-S micro (Waters Corporation, USA).

Nb2C-MXene were prepared by an HF-based etching method [31]. In brief, 1.0 g of Nb2AlC powder were slowly added in 10 min to 40 mL of HF (40 wt% aqueous solution) with magnetic stirring by a magnetic heating stirrer. The mixture solution was stirred at 60 °C for 24 h and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The precipitates were added to 10 mL of HF (40 wt% aqueous solution) and ultrasonicated for 13 h with an ultrasonic cleaning machine (KQ-500DE, Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd.). The suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and then repeatedly washed with ultrapure water until the pH value of the supernatant was about 7. The collected precipitates were frozen at −80°C for 12 h, followed by a freeze-drying process to gain solid-state Nb2C. The Nb2C powder was stored at 4°C in a nitrogen atmosphere for further use.

Blue fluorescence emission CDs (B-CDs) were synthesized based on a previously reported one-step hydrothermal reaction method with slight modifications [32]. Briefly, 0.11 g of L-Glutamic acid and 0.16 g of o-phenylenediamine were dispersed and thoroughly mixed in 25 mL of formamide. The mixture was transferred to a 50-mL Teflon-equipped stainless autoclave with heating at 200°C for 10 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitates. The collected supernatant was filtered by a 0.22-μm filter membrane and then dialyzed against ultrapure water for 24 hours using a dialysis bag (MW: 1000 Da) for further purification. The solid-state B-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying and store at 4℃ away from light.

The B-CDs@Apt conjugation was achieved via a well-established carbodiimide method [33]. Briefly, 100 μL of B-CDs (1 mg mL−1) were added to 100 μL of fresh mixture of EDC (5 mg mL−1) and NHS (5 mg mL−1) in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4), followed with sonication at room temperature for 30 min. Then, 20-μL aliquots of the above mixture were mixed with 80 μL of different concentrations of amino-modified aptamers. After incubation at 37°C for 12 hours, the reaction solution was ultrafiltered using an ultrafiltration tube (MW = 30 kDa) to remove the unconjugated B-CDs. The ultrafiltered B-CDs@Apt was redissolved in 20 μL of ultrapure water as B-CDs@Apt solution and stored at 4℃ away from light.

Prior to determinate CAP, 10 μL of Nb2C-MXene solution (1.5 mg mL−1), 10 μL of B-CDs@Apt solution, and 10 μL of 10 × binding buffer (200 mM Tris-HCl, 1000 mM NaCl, 10.0 mM CaCl2⋅2 H2O, 50.0 mM MgCl2⋅6 H2O, 50.0 mM KCl, pH = 8) were added to 50 μL of Milli-Q water, and then incubated for 25 min at 37°C in the dark with horizontal shaking. With the addition of 20 μL of CAP solution with different concentrations (0–160 ng mL−1), the solution was incubated at 37°C in the dark with continuous shaking for another 25 min. The fluorescence spectra of the reaction solution were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

For selectivity assay, other antibiotics including AMP, NOR, ROX, STR, VAN, ERY, NFZ, FZD, and NFT were tested as possible interference substances. For stability study, the developed aptasensor was evaluated by determining the fluorescence response of the same batch of the detection system over a period of 7 days for different concentrations of CAP at 0, 5, 50 and 150 ng mL−1.

For solid-phase detection, small round pieces of filter paper were obtained using a 4-mm puncher. The mixture of Nb2C-MXene (150 μg mL−1) and B-CDs@Apt (2 μmol L−1) was prepared as mentioned above and incubated at 37°C for 25 min in the dark. Then, 5 μL of the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt mixture was dropped onto each small piece of filter paper. After the probe being dried at room temperature, the paper-based sensor was ready for use, or it could be stored at 4℃ away from light for future use. During the detection process, 5 μL of sample was dropped on the test paper and the fluorescence image was recorded under an ultraviolet (UV) light of 365 nm using a mobile phone.

For method extension study, the anti-OTC aptamer was labeled with green fluorescence carbon dots (G-CDs@Apt) using the carbodiimide method. For OTC detection in tube, after optimization, 10 μL of Nb2C-MXene solution (1.5 mg mL−1), 10 μL of G-CDs@Apt solution (20 μmol L−1), and 10 μL of 10 × binding buffer were added to 50 μL of ultrapure water and preincubated for 25 min at 37°C in the dark with horizontal shaking. After being added with 20 μL of sample, the mixture was incubated at 37°C in the dark with continuous shaking for another 25 min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 470 nm. For OTC detection using a PAD, the Nb2C-MXene (15 μg mL−1) and G-CDs@Apt (2 μmol L−1) was prepared similarly in 100 μL of 1 × binding buffer and preincubated at 37°C for 25 min in the dark. Then, 5 μL of the Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt probe was dropped onto each small piece of MCE paper and dried at room temperature before use. When 5 μL of sample was added on the test paper, the fluorescence image was recorded under UV light (365 nm) using a mobile phone. For duplex detection on the PAD, after optimization, the CAP test paper was loaded with 5 μL of a mixture of Nb2C-MXene (150 μg mL−1) and B-CDs@Apt (2 μmol L−1), whereas the OTC test paper was loaded with 5 μL of a mixture of Nb2C-MXene (150 μg mL−1) and G-CDs@Apt (6 μmol L−1).

Shrimp and fish samples were purchased from a local market in Tianjin, China. For testing the aptasensor in tube, the aquatic samples were pre-treated according to a standard protocol with slight modifications for subsequent different analytical methods [34]. Briefly, the fish or shrimp samples were peeled, deboned, and chopped with a meat grinder. Next, 10 g of grinded samples were spiked with different concentrations of CAP standards and homogenized well. To extract CAP from each sample, 10 mL of ethyl acetate was added. The mixture was vortexed vigorously for 10 min and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 minutes at 4°C. The supernatant was collected, and the precipitates were extracted once again to collect supernatant. Subsequently, the two collected supernatants were mixed and blow-dried by nitrogen. To remove salt ions from samples for LC–MS/MS analysis, the dried extract was dissolved in 3 mL of acetonitrile and processed through a solid-phase extraction column followed by elution with 4 mL of methanol. Finally, the eluate was blow-dried with nitrogen and dissolved in 1 mL of PBS buffer or 1 mL of acetonitrile for subsequent analysis using the developed aptasensor or LC–MS/MS method, respectively. For testing the aptasensor on PAD, the codfish samples were pre-treated with a reduced protocol. Briefly, grinded samples were spiked with different concentrations of CAP standards and OTC standards. Then, 10 mL of extraction buffer (27.6 mM Na2CO3, 22.6 mM NaHCO3, pH=8) was added to 10 g of the spiked samples, followed by vortex and extraction for 10 minutes. Subsequently, the samples were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and filtered by a 0.45-μm filter membrane for the following detection on PADs.

Fig. 1. depicts the fabrication and working principle of the signal-on fluorescent aptasensor and the paper-based analytical device based on Nb2C-MXene and B-CDs@Apt. As shown in Fig. 1a, Nb2C-MXene was prepared using Nb2AlC MAX powder as the precursor. After removal of the aluminum (Al) layers by HF etching and further ultrasonication, the obtained Nb2C-MXene material has a structure with high specific surface area composed of multilayered nanosheets loosely stacked. On the other hand, carboxy-containing B-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal approach with L-Glutamic acid and o-phenylenediamine as feedstocks and then coupled with anti-CAP aptamer via the carbodiimide crosslinking method. Afterward, Nb2C-MXene (nanoquencher) and carbon dots-functionalized aptamer (B-CDs@Apt, fluorescent recognition element) consist of the signal-on aptasensor. The sensing strategy is shown in Fig. 1b. In the absence of target CAP, B-CDs@Apt adsorbs on the surface of Nb2C-MXene material through interaction and the aptasensor exhibits a fluorescent "off" state. When CAP is present in the detection system, it is specifically recognized by B-CDs@Apt and forms an aptamer/analyte complex, and the accompanying conformational change results in the detachment of B-CDs@Apt from Nb2C-MXene. Thus, the quenching effect blocked by the increased distance between the B-CDs and the Nb2C-MXene leads to a fluorescence "turn-on" of the aptasensor.

More favorably, the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system can be easily loaded on a paper-based platform. Fig. 1c depicts the microscopic process during the paper-based detection. Take advantage of the multilayer nanosheet stack-like MXene nanostructure, abundant B-CDs@Apt probes can be loaded per unit area of MXene distribution region, thereby increasing analyte access to the probes. The channels between the nanolayers also improve mass transfer efficiency and allow easy release of the analyte/B-CDs@Apt complexes into the surroundings microenvironment. When the sample solution dries, the target/B-CDs@Apt complexes can fall in the surrounding MXene-free region, showing a fluorescence "turn-on" state. Fig. 1d shows the advantages in principle of the multilayer nanoquencher system over traditional nanosheet nanoquenchers. When the nanoquencher/aptamer system immobilized on a test paper, the usage of nanoquencher is limited because the target-released aptamer probe cannot be allowed to fall back onto the nanoquencher region. Traditional monolayer nanoquencher tend to cover a large area on the surface of the test paper, leading to fluorescence quenching of the falling back probes. The multilayer MXene can carry abundant aptamer probes on limited region for analyte access and reaction, providing outstanding advantages over other nanoquenchers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Nb2C-MXene as multilayer nanoquencher
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. S1a and Fig. 2f, the SEM image of the Nb2AlC precursor exhibited irregular blocky morphology. After etching with HF, the MXene showed a loose accordion-like structure with visible gaps between the stacked layers (Fig. 2a and S1b), indicating that the Al layers were stripped during the etching process. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum of Nb2AlC precursor exhibited the presence of niobium (Nb), Al, carbon (C), and oxygen (O) elements, whereas the spectrum of Nb2C-MXene revealed a markedly reduced signal for Al element (Fig. S1c–d and Table S1). The element distribution mapping images of Nb2C-MXene and Nb2AlC precursor were given in Fig. 2b–e and Fig. 2g, respectively, which further displayed the loss of the aluminum layers. Moreover, PXRD patterns of Nb2AlC and Nb2C were shown in Fig. 2h. The strong diffraction peak (104) of Nb2AlC at
θ = 38.9 °
disappeared almost completely in the PXRD pattern of Nb2C, indicating the removal of Al from the precursor. The left-shifting and broadening of the diffraction peak (002) at
θ = 12.8 °
indicated that MAX phase was converted into MXene phase [35]. The UV–vis spectra data (Fig. 2i) showed that the Nb2C-MXene in aqueous suspension (0.15 mg mL−1) had a broad absorption from 200 nm to 700 nm, indicating that it can be used as an excellent fluorescence acceptor for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [36]. These results suggest that the multilayer Nb2C-MXene material has been successfully synthesized and that it has the potential to be an effective nanoquencher in signal-on fluorescence sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomodification engineering of aptamer with zero-dimensional carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The shape and size of B-CDs were characterized by HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 3a–b, B-CDs were approximately spherical and uniformly distributed, with an average diameter of approximately 2.22 nm. The inset in Fig. 3a showed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) lattice distance of the graphitic carbon, indicating that the B-CDs contain graphite-like structure [37]. The optical properties of the B-CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3c). B-CDs had a broad absorption band with two peaks λ
max1 = 266 nm and λ
max2 = 310 nm, corresponding to the π−π* transitions of CC bonds in carbon cores [38]. The inset in Fig. 3c indicated that the B-CDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1) was clear and transparent in the daylight, and exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). The compositions of B-CDs were investigated by FT-IR spectrometry and XPS. Comparative analysis of the FT-IR spectra of B-CDs (Fig. 3d) and their feedstocks (Fig. 3e–f) revealed that B-CDs possessed a strong absorption peak at 1628.6 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO, and a broad peak near 3300 cm−1, which may be corresponding to the stretching vibration of O−H [39]. As shown in Fig. 3g, the full XPS spectra indicated that the surface elements of the B-CDs primarily contained C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), and O1s (530 eV). In the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 3h–j), the C1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three binding energy peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.2 eV, which correspond to C–N, C–O, and CO, respectively. The N1s curve displayed three peaks, attributed to pyridinic N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic N (399.0 eV), and graphitic N (399.8 eV), respectively. The O1s band contained two peaks at 531.1 eV and 532.8 eV for CO, C–O, respectively. These results indicate that the synthesized B-CDs have carboxyl groups and can be used to label aptamer probes. Moreover, the B-CDs can serve as good FRET donors due to their smaller radius (1.11 ± 0.12 nm).

The fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs at different excitation wavelengths were analyzed, and the result showed that the optimal excitation wavelength was 350 nm (Fig. S2), which is compatible with the common 365 nm UV lamp. Analyzing the fluorescence spectra of aqueous solutions of B-CDs at different concentrations, the maximum fluorescence intensity was reached at a concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1, while the weakening of fluorescence emission above this concentration could be attributed to the aggregation-caused quenching phenomenon (Fig. S3). Thus, 0.5 mg mL−1 was chosen as the working concentration of B-CDs for labeling the anti-CAP aptamers to ensure that B-CDs were in excess but not aggregated. Besides, the coupling of B-CDs with aptamer was analyzed via zeta potential measurement. As shown in Fig. 4a, the zeta potential of B-CDs and B-CDs@Apt were measured to be −3.93 and −18.41 mV, indicating the B-CDs had successfully modified with negatively charged aptamers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a multilayer MXene aptasensor based on FRET
ce:section.ce:para: Since the strong absorption band of Nb2C-MXene (Fig. 2i) overlaps with the fluorescence emission band of B-CDs (Fig. 3c), we tried to build a FRET sensor by leveraging the Nb2C-MXene as an acceptor and the B-CD as a donor. The sensing strategy is to control the donor–acceptor distance by analyte-induced conformational changes in the aptamer. Just as MXene was reported to be able to adsorb nucleic acids efficiently [22,40], the adsorption capacity of Nb2C-MXene to B-CDs@Apt was verified by zeta potential measurement. As shown in Fig. 4a, the zeta potential of Nb2C-MXene mixed with B-CDs@Apt was −36.11 mV, which was more negative than that of Nb2C-MXene (−27.27 mV) or B-CDs@Apt (−18.41 mV). This result indicates the formation of Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt complexes, implying the presence of other forms of adsorption interactions such as hydrogen bonds, coordination bonds and van der Waals forces to counteract electrostatic repulsion [41]. Next, the effect of Nb2C-MXene on the fluorescence emission of B-CDs@Apt was evaluated by fluorescence spectroscopy measurements. When mixed with Nb2C-MXene, the B-CDs@Apt exhibited significantly attenuated emission (Fig. 4b). The phenomenon can be explained by the fluorescence quenching effect based on FRET. In comparison, mixing B-CDs with Nb2C-MXene did not significantly alter the fluorescence emission of B-CDs (Fig. 4c), suggesting that the adsorption capacity of Nb2C-MXene is mainly targeted at the DNA fraction rather than the carbon dot fraction. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity of the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system was significantly restored with the addition of CAP (Fig. 4b). This phenomenon is mainly due to the conformational change of B-CDs@Apt upon specific binding to CAP, resulting in the escape of B-CDs@Apt from the adsorption of Nb2C-MXene and the consequent recovery of fluorescence. In addition, the conformational change of the B-CDs@Apt upon encountering the analyte may itself increase the distance between the B-CDs and the Nb2C-MXene. According to the average radius of B-CDs (1.11 nm) and conformational modeling of the aptamer (Fig. S4), the acceptor-donor spacing for MXene contact probes is approximately 1–5 nm, which is likely to be less than the Förster distance for FRET [42]. When the analyte was added to filter paper covered with an excess of Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt complex (30 ×), recovered fluorescence signals were clearly observed in the periphery of the loading zone and in some crack zone under UV light (Fig. 4d). However, more completely black areas on the paper sheet were distinguished from the fluorescent areas, suggesting that the activated B-CDs@Apt/CAP complexes that fell back onto the MXene nanoquencher-containing region were still fluorescence-off. These results verify that the fluorescence recovery phenomenon originates from the analyte-induced escape of the B-CDs@Apt probe from the Nb2C-MXene (Fig. 4d).

Several experimental parameters were optimized, including aptamer concentration, Nb2C concentration, pH of the buffer, incubation time, and incubation temperature. First, different concentrations of aptamer were used for labelling 0.5 mg mL−1 of B-CDs and the fluorescence spectra of the obtained B-CDs@Apt were measured. As displayed in Figs. S5a, 5 μmol L−1 of the aptamer was most suitable for the labelling reaction because the fluorescence intensity reached the peak level. Since the concentration of nanoquencher is crucial for constructing the fluorescence sensing system, the quenching effect of Nb2C-MXene at different concentration on the B-CDs@Apt was evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 4e, the fluorescence intensities of the system decreased gradually with increasing Nb2C concentration from 0.1 to 2.0 mg mL−1 and stabilized at 1.5 mg mL−1. Therefore, 1.5 mg mL−1 of Nb2C-MXene was conducive to the quenching reaction, and the concentration was used for further experiments. Next, the pH of the buffer was optimized by monitoring the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs, B-CDs@Apt, and Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system at different pH values. Fig. 4f indicates that the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs and B-CDs@Apt were highest at a pH of 8. Coincidentally, the best quenching effect of Nb2C-MXene on B-CDs@Apt was also observed at pH = 8. Therefore, pH = 8 was chosen as the optimal acid-base condition for the sensing system. It could be seen in Fig. S5b that the fluorescence quenching effect of Nb2C-MXene on B-CDs@Apt enhanced with time, and at 25 min the fluorescence intensity decreased to a low enough level for the subsequent analysis. Since the efficiency of the aptamer-target binding event directly affects the fluorescence response, the incubation time for fluorescence recovery reaction after adding the CAP (300 ng mL−1) was further optimized. As presented in Fig. 4g, the recovery of fluorescence intensity increased as the incubation time increased from 5 to 25 min and reached the highest level at 25 min. Taking the overall process time into account, the incubation time for both the fluorescence quenching reaction and the fluorescence recovery reaction was set to be 25 min. Finally, we investigated the effect of incubation temperature on the fluorescence recovery performance of Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system after the addition of the target, and the results showed that the optimal incubation temperature was 37℃ (Fig. 4h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity, anti-interference, and repeatability of the multilayer MXene aptasensor in tube
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimized experimental conditions, the analytical performance of the proposed aptasensor was evaluated by quantifying different concentrations of CAP standard solutions. As shown in Fig. 4i, with the increase of CAP concentration of standard solutions, the fluorescence signal of the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system gradually increased, suggesting that the aptamer-CAP binding event hindered the adsorption of Nb2C-MXene to B-CDs@Apt and disrupted the fluorescence quenching effect. Importantly, the concentration range of 1–160 ng mL−1 exhibited a good linear relationship (
y = 2552.45165 + 14.05806 x
,
R
2
= 0.997
), where x and y represent the concentration of the CAP and the fluorescence intensity of the system, respectively (Fig. 4j). The limit of detection (LOD, 3σ/S) and limit of quantification (LOQ, 10σ/S) were calculated to be 0.285 ng mL−1 and 0.95 ng mL−1, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution (
n = 10
) and S is the slope of the calibration curve [43]. As displayed in Table S2, our aptasensor exhibits satisfactory performance in the quantitative analysis of CAP with a wide linear range and high sensitivity compared with other approaches. To verify the specificity of the aptasensor, selectivity assay was conducted by comparing the signal responses towards CAP and typical antibiotics including ampicillin (AMP), erythromycin (ERY), norfloxacin (NOR), roxithromycin (ROX), streptomycin (STR), vancomycin (VAN), nitrofurazone (NFZ), nitrofurantoin (NFT), and furazolidone (FZD). The concentration of CAP was set at 150 ng mL−1, and that concentration of other antibiotics was 1.5 μg mL−1. As shown in Fig. 4k, only CAP significantly induced the fluorescence recovery response of the aptasensor, whereas other interfering antibiotics did not cause significant changes in fluorescence intensity signal, which indicated that the developed aptasensor had excellent selectivity. To evaluate the stability, a series of B-CDs@Apt solution were prepared and stored at 4℃ away from light to monitor the fluorescent intensity every day in a week. As shown in Fig. S6, it was found that B-CDs@Apt solution exhibited satisfactory storage stability. During these days, the inter-day relative standard deviation (RSD) values of the method were calculated to be 1.3–2.8 % (Fig. 4l), which indicates that the method is precise enough.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Appling the multilayer MXene aptasensor to analyze shrimp and fish samples
Development of a paper-based platform based on the multilayer MXene aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para:
We further explored the potential of this technology for point-of-care (POC) applications by performing a solid-phase detection study on the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system. First, we dropped the reaction mixture of Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt and CAP onto the surface of a black plate, and found that the increase in fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) was visible and proportional to the concentration of CAP (Fig. S8). Next, we attempted to fabricate a fluorescent paper-based analytical device (PAD) as illustrated in Fig. 6a. The PAD can be prepared ahead of time by simply loading the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt mixture onto small round pieces of filter paper. Notably, the consumption of Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt material can be greatly reduced. After screening the material of the filter paper, cellulose-derived films were found to have good loading property for Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt probes (Fig. 6b–c, S9 and S10). More importantly, we found that the reddish fluorescent background of mixed cellulose ester (MCE) film can efficiently enhance the visual distinction between blank and CAP samples even for low concentration CAP samples (Fig. 6b). This phenomenon is also supported by the results of the ImageJ analysis. The ratio of the blue channel to the red channel (B/R value) was proportional to the concentration of CAP. The B/R value showed a good linear relationship with the logarithmic (log10) CAP concentration (
y = 1.7615 + 0.3578 x
,
R
2
= 0.993
), ranging from 5 ng mL−1 to 100 ng mL−1 (Fig. 6c). The LOD was calculated to be 0.360 ng mL−1. Attributed to their multilayer nanosheet stack-like nanostructures, MXene nanoblocks distributed at low densities on the paper substrate (Fig. 6d and S11) can carry an abundance of fluorescent aptamers, providing more sufficient opportunities for analyte-aptamer contact. The numerous channels between the nanosheets varied in size, with large channels spaced more than 100 nm apart (Fig. 6e–f), facilitating the passage of the aptamer/analyte complexes. The release of the fluorescent aptamer/analyte complexes may be related to its electrostatic repulsion with MXene, and the low-density distribution of MXene allowed the released fluorescent complexes to easily fall into the MXene-free region (Fig. 6f). In contrast, when we loaded monolayer Nb2C-MXene nanosheets with the same mass concentration (750 ng/12.5 mm2) on MCE films, the fluorescence signal was weak with or without the addition of CAP (Fig. 6g). After reducing the amount of Nb2C on the MCE films, many B-CDs@Apt probes were not adsorbed by the monolayer Nb2C nanoquencher, resulting in high fluorescence regardless of the presence or absence of the analyte (Fig. 6h). In addition, the response time of the PAD to the analyte was tested. Rapid fluorescence change was observed after sample addition, with a response time of about 5 minutes (Fig. S12). Therefore, the developed Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt paper-based platform opens an avenue for easy, low-cost, on-site, and rapid detection methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scalability study of the multilayer MXene paper-based aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: In light of the satisfactory performance of the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt system for on-site detection, we further extended its application to the detection of another analyte to validate the versatility of the sensing strategy. As illustrated in Fig. 7a, we modified the anti-OTC aptamer (anti-OTC Apt) with green fluorescence carbon dots (G-CDs) to replace the recognition probe in detection system. G-CDs and G-CDs@Apt had optimal fluorescence emission at about 535 nm (Fig. S13). Zeta potential data indicated the nanomodification was successful (Fig. S14). Another experiment in which supernatant fluorescence was measured after centrifugation verified that the Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt system is formed by the binding of Nb2C-MXene and the aptamer component (Fig. S15). The binding mechanism can be explained by intermolecular interaction such as hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces, and coordination bonds between MXene and ssDNA [22,41]. The fluorescence response of the Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt system to OTC was validated as shown in Fig. 7b. After optimizing the experimental conditions (Fig. S16), the fluorescence signal of the detection system in tube was linearly and positively correlated with the OTC concentration in the range of 0–100 ng mL−1 (Fig. 7c–d). The LOD was calculated to be 0.399 ng mL−1. We also established a ready-to-use PAD for OTC detection by loading the Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt system onto the MCE film. Distinguishable from the blank control, the PAD spiked with OTC samples showed green fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 7e). As shown in Fig. 7f, the G/R value showed a linear relationship with the logarithmic (log10) OTC concentration ranging from 1 ng mL−1 to 60 ng mL−1. The LOD was calculated to be 0.867 ng mL−1.

Next, we compared multilayler Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt and monolayer Nb2C/G-CDs@Apt in the construction of paper-based OTC biosensor. Fig. S17a showed fluorescence quenching efficiency of G-CDs@Apt by monolayer Nb2C on MCE paper (12.5 mm2). Compared to multilayer Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt (75 ng/10 pmol) quenching system, 150 ng of monolayer Nb2C was sufficient to completely quench the fluorescence of G-CDs@Apt (10 pmol), while 75 ng of monolayer Nb2C only accomplished a partial quenching of the fluorescence. Although 37.5 ng of monolayer Nb2C was sufficient to quench the fluorescence of G-CDs@Apt (5 pmol), lowering the amount of G-CDs@Apt tended to reduce the maximum fluorescence recovery and the upper limit of the detection range. Therefore, we further evaluated the detection sensitivity of monolayer Nb2C/G-CDs@Apt sensors (loading 75 ng/10 pmol dose or 150 ng/10 pmol dose on a 12.5 mm2 area) on MCE paper (Fig. S17b–d). As expected, these monolayer Nb2C/G-CDs@Apt paper-based sensors exhibited lower detection sensitivity, smaller maximum fluorescence recovery, and narrower detection range than the multilayer Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt paper-based sensor. Similar to multilayer Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt test paper, multilayer Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt test paper also showed a fast response (5–25 min) to OTC targets (Fig. S18). This advantage meets the demands for rapid detection in field application compared with other established analytical methods (Table S2).

The specificity result of the PADs was shown in Fig. 7g–h and Fig.S19, revealing that only specific targets and relevant mixture had strong output signal, whereas the detection signals of other antibiotics and interfering substances such as protein, fat, and mineral were relatively weak. Moreover, the sensor's stability under various storage conditions was evaluated. Our synthesized Nb2C-MXene showed long-term storage stability under nitrogen protection (Fig. S20a). The Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt stored in solution for 30 days also maintained performance for paper-based detection (Fig. S20b). As shown in Fig. S20c–d, inert gas protection and humidification were critical for the preservation of PADs, but even under the current optimal storage conditions (4℃, nitrogen protection and humidification), there was a visual color difference between the stored PAD and the freshly prepared PAD. These results also suggest that oxidation of MXene may inhibit its binding to aptamers (Fig. S20d). In addition, we evaluated the effect of different coating methods on the performance of the sensors. As shown in Fig. S21a–b, non-uniform distribution of MXene on MCE paper (repeated drops at the same location) greatly reduced the detection sensitivity. Furthermore, a single dropping of the probe and multiple dropping of the diluted probe had little effect on probe uniformity and detection performance, but repeated dropping and drying operations may cause MXene to be oxidized due to prolonged exposure (Fig. S21c–d). Subsequently, codfish spiked samples were used here to validate the reliability of the fluorescent PADs. As shown in Table S4, the proposed method had recoveries from 106.53 % to 116.20 % and RSD from 1.53 % to 9.62 %. In addition, we have found that MXene/CDs@Apt paper-based sensing strategies can also be used for on-situ detection of biomacromolecules such as the SARS-CoV-2 spike proteins (Fig. S22), where all that needs to be done is to replace the aptamer.

As shown in the fluorescence spectra of the two probes under 365 nm excitation (Fig. 6i), green fluorescence and blue fluorescence are relatively balanced when the ratio between the concentrations of B-CDs@Apt and G-CDs@Apt was 1:3–1:5. Therefore, we conducted a duplex detection assay for CAP and OTC on a paper-based platform (Fig. 6j). The developed PAD consisted of two types of MXene/CDs@Apt papers, which were loaded with B-CDs@Apt and G-CDs@Apt, respectively. The loading ratio between B-CDs@Apt and G-CDs@Apt was 1:3. As shown in Fig. 6k–l, the duplex detection of CAP and OTC was achieved by simultaneous observation of the fluorescence of the two types of test paper under UV light (365 nm). When negative, CAP-positive, OTC-positive, and double-positive samples were added to the PAD, only the CAP-containing samples could effectively enhance the blue fluorescence of the Nb2C-MXene/B-CDs@Apt paper. Similarly, only the OTC-containing samples were effective in enhancing the green fluorescence of the Nb2C-MXene/G-CDs@Apt paper. These results suggest that the MXene/CDs@Apt sensing strategy has good scalability and has the potential to further enable multiplex detection on paper-based platforms.

In summary, we developed a signal-on, scalable, and robust approach for on-site quantitative analysis based on multilayer MXene nanoquencher and CDs-labeled aptamer probe. Specifically, highly luminescent CDs with blue fluorescence emission were prepared using a hydrothermal method and then surface-functionalized with the anti-CAP aptamer, denoted as B-CDs@Apt probe. Nb2C-MXene with loose accordion-like nanostructure was synthesized as an excellent nanoquencher for B-CDs@Apt sensing, which can efficiently quench the fluorescence by adsorbing the DNA component of B-CDs@Apt. Upon the addition of analyte CAP, conformational change induced by aptamer/target recognition can lead to the release of the B-CDs@Apt probes from the Nb2C-MXene nanoquencher. Therefore, the concentration of CAP can be determined by analyzing the increased fluorescence intensity in a signal-on mode. Selective and sensitive quantification of CAP was achieved in the range of 1–160 ng mL−1 and detection limit of 0.285 ng mL−1. The method showed impressive storage stability and reproducibility with inter-day relative standard deviation in the range of 1.3–2.8 %. This robust aptasensor has been successfully applied to analyze shrimp and fish samples and achieved satisfactory results. More importantly, the multilayer Nb2C-MXene can be easily loaded onto MCE filter papers, carrying numerous B-CDs@Apt probes even at a low-density distribution. The developed PAD allowed for a rapid response of 5 min towards CAP with a LOD of 0.360 ng mL−1. Furthermore, the MXene/carbon dots-based aptasensor can be easily converted to detect OTC by replacing the B-CDs@Apt probe to a G-CDs@Apt probe. The LODs for in-tube detection and paper-based detection were 0.399 and 0.867 ng mL−1, respectively. The duplex detection assay has shown that CAP and OTC can be analyzed simultaneously under UV light using a coupled PAD. Obviously, the MXene/CDs-based sensing technology herein has promising prospects, and our future research may focus on the target extension for the MXene/CDs-based platform by introducing multicolor CDs functionalized with aptamers for other analytes.
